story_summary: A KPMG leader arrives in Philadelphia (1991), confronts a resistant partner culture and weak client relationships, uses external assessment and direct relationship-building to turn the office around while managing firm-wide compensation reform and personal family health challenges.

context.role: Managing partner, Philadelphia office; later national managing partner, financial services line
context.stakes: Turnaround of a large regional office (35 partners, 350+ staff), major client relationships, firm-wide partner compensation fairness, personal family health (wife's cancer)

core_conflict: Fix a large, insular, resistant Philadelphia office and weak client relationships while implementing firm-wide compensation reform and balancing expanding regional/national responsibilities and a serious family health crisis.

decision_moments.trigger: Unable to accurately assess partner skills amid insularity and entrenched culture.
constraints: Partners' extreme inward focus and loss of objectivity
Rambunctious, ornery partner behavior and resistance to change
options: Rely solely on personal judgment and internal feedback
Engage external experts for objective assessment
decision_moments.choice: Hired RHR International to assess key partners and advise on cultural issues.
decision_moments.reasoning: The external psychologists had a strong reputation and could provide objective, professional diagnosis and recommendations to cut through internal noise.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Potential partner backlash from external scrutiny and disruption of existing responsibilities.
decision_moments.outcome: Their work produced sweeping realignment of responsibilities, rapid attitudinal shifts and improved productivity.

decision_moments.trigger: Partners insisted protocol prevented meeting the CEO of the firm's largest client, asserting he was uninterested.
constraints: Local partners' adherence to protocol and gatekeeping
Partners' lack of existing top-level client relationships
options: Accept partners' protocol and meet lower-level client contacts first
Press partners to introduce CEO (relying on their connections)
Contact the CEO directly to establish relationship
decision_moments.choice: Called the CEO of Rohm and Haas directly and introduced himself as the new managing partner.
decision_moments.reasoning: To test the partners' assumption, establish direct executive relationships, and avoid being limited by internal immaturity or excuses.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Risk of embarrassing partners or being rebuffed by the CEO.
decision_moments.outcome: The CEO invited him to dinner, became a regular professional friend and attended audit committee meetings thereafter.

decision_moments.trigger: A major client, Pete Musser, requested a $10,000 donation and suggested membership in the Alexis de Tocqueville Society.
constraints: Expectation of philanthropic contribution tied to access
Personal norms about political and civic giving (and prior advice)
options: Donate and engage in the society
decision_moments.choice: Sent the $10,000 donation and accepted the resulting society membership and board appointment.
decision_moments.reasoning: Following prior advice and recognizing philanthropic engagement as a pathway to build relationships and presence in the city.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Financial outlay and perception of quid pro quo.
decision_moments.outcome: Appointment launched a series of new relationships and additional civic board roles.

decision_moments.trigger: Solicitation by Ed Rendell and invitation to meet while new to town.
constraints: Being new to the city and unfamiliar with the politician
decision_moments.choice: Met with Ed Rendell for breakfast, later agreed to serve on his management and productivity board.
decision_moments.reasoning: To proactively build meaningful relationships and leverage civic engagement to meet key city leaders.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Perceived involvement in local politics and time commitment.
decision_moments.outcome: Gained access to key movers and shakers; Ed maintained ongoing personal engagement and later became mayor and governor.

decision_moments.trigger: Firm tasked him to overhaul a controversial, unfair partner compensation system affecting 1,500 partners.
constraints: Large scale (1,500 partners) and political sensitivity
options: Make minor adjustments to existing system
Design a comprehensive, iterative realignment and present to management committee
decision_moments.choice: Developed and iterated a new compensation allocation process and implemented it firm-wide.
decision_moments.reasoning: Address systemic unfairness through data-driven iterations to achieve a dramatically improved, defensible allocation.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Political resistance from partners and potential disruption to incomes.
decision_moments.outcome: Management committee unanimously approved and immediately implemented the recommendations; considered a career-best contribution.

decision_moments.trigger: 1993 firm-wide reorganization creating national practice units and a new role opening.
constraints: Leaving local Philadelphia and area managing-partner responsibilities
Accepting a demanding national role amid other commitments
options: Accept the national managing partner role
decision_moments.choice: Accepted appointment as national managing partner for the new financial services line of business.
decision_moments.reasoning: Though surprised, he took on the larger organizational challenge presented by the reorganization.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Two of the most challenging years of his career and relinquishing local ties.
decision_moments.outcome: Transitioned to national role and received recognition and a presentation dinner from local partners on departure.

principles.principle: Use independent, professional assessment to diagnose talent and culture when internal objectivity is compromised.
principles.evidence: I hired RHR to conduct assessments of the key partners and assist me in coming to grips with the troublesome cultural issues.

principles.principle: Initiate relationships directly; don't let gatekeeping or protocol prevent access to senior client executives.
principles.evidence: Never sell yourself short and never be bashful about establishing relationships.

principles.principle: Move decisively and rapidly when leading a turnaround; bold, timely action can accelerate change.
principles.evidence: I made bold moves, and I made them rapidly.

principles.principle: Leverage civic and philanthropic engagement to build networks and open doors in a new community.
principles.evidence: That move launched a series of new relationships for me in the city.

principles.principle: Address systemic unfairness with iterative, data-driven redesigns to gain credible, implementable solutions.
principles.evidence: Following iteration after iteration, and revisions upon revisions, I finally arrived at a process and income allocation that was a dramatic improvement.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When internal bias and noise obscure talent assessment, engage established external experts to provide objective evaluations.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I hired RHR to conduct assessments of the key partners and assist me in coming to grips with the troublesome cultural issues.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: If local gatekeepers block access to senior stakeholders, contact the stakeholder directly to test assumptions and build the relationship.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I tapped my phone and asked my secretary to place a call to his office

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Prefer bold, rapid interventions over slow incrementalism in turnarounds: act quickly and iterate based on results.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I made bold moves, and I made them rapidly.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Use visible philanthropic or civic actions to create introductions and credibility in a new community.
decision_heuristics.evidence: By the way, Keith, I need to get a $10,000 check from you.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: For politically sensitive, large-scale redesigns, iterate models repeatedly until you reach a defensible, improved allocation and seek unanimous governance approval.
decision_heuristics.evidence: Following iteration after iteration, and revisions upon revisions, I finally arrived at a process and income allocation that was a dramatic improvement.

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Moderate-high; willing to accept political and relational risk for decisive change
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Direct, proactive, relationship-focused; personally initiates senior contacts
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Hands-on, ownership-driven; accepts firm-wide responsibilities and leads implementation
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Pragmatic and results-oriented, values reciprocity and civic engagement as legitimate means to build influence

quotes.quote: I plunged in with all of my energy.
I made bold moves, and I made them rapidly.
Never sell yourself short and never be bashful about establishing relationships.
There are all sorts of quid pro quos in life, and they can contribute to success if one plays his cards smartly.
